creator: matt@tnpi.net
text:
Alternatively, improve the behavior of mysql_confdir and/or mysql_optfile settings.

I expected that if I set mysql_confdir=/mnt/etc, then mysqld would get its configuration from /mnt/etc/my.cnf. Instead, what mysqld effectively starts with is '--defaults-file=/usr/local/etc/mysql/my.cnf --defaults-extra-file=/mnt/etc/my.cnf'.  The same goes for setting mysql_optfile.

From reading the comments in rc.d/mysql-server, there is not even a suggestion that /usr/local/etc/mysql/my.cnf is ALWAYS read first (a POLA violation). Once I figured that out, it was straight forward to find the problematic settings in /usr/local/etc/mysql/my.cnf and override them in the extras file.

Another option: deprecate both of those config settings, especially mysql_confdir because it doesn't map to any notion that mysqld has, and make explicit rc.d settings that map directly to the mysql options. IE, something like this:

sysrc mysql_defaults_file=/mnt/etc/my.cnf  (Default: /usr/local/etc/mysql/my.cnf

sysrc mysql_defaults_extra_file=/mnt/etc/extra.cnf (Default: empty)

Provide a shim to continue working for existing values in /etc/rc.conf. Add an explanation in UPGRADING to the effect: "Previous versions of the mysql-server startup script provided no mechanism to choose a primary my.cnf file, only offering an 'extra' file that could contain overrides to the primary /usr/local/etc/mysql/my.cnf file. You can retain the old behavior by setting only mysql_defaults_extra_file to the same value as the legacy mysql_optfile setting."
